title: Bipolar Thought Record
description: Use this worksheet to examine thoughts that arise during mood episodes — both depressive and elevated. The goal is to check whether your thinking is being colored by your current mood state, and to find a more balanced perspective. This is especially important in bipolar disorder, where mood episodes can powerfully distort how we see ourselves, others, and the future.
instructions: When you notice a strong mood shift (high or low), work through each step below. If you can't do it in the moment, fill it in afterward — even hours or days later. Over time, you'll start to recognize your personal patterns of mood-colored thinking.

fields.label: 1. What happened? (Stick to observable facts)
fields.placeholder: e.g., I woke up at 4 AM feeling full of energy and started planning a major home renovation project

fields.label: 2. Current mood state — Are you in a depressive, elevated, or mixed episode?

options.label: Depressed — low mood, low energy, withdrawal
Mildly depressed — somewhat low but functioning
Mildly elevated — more energy and optimism than usual
Mixed — elements of both depression and elevation

fields.label: 3. The hot thought — What went through your mind?
fields.placeholder: e.g., 'This is the best idea I've ever had — I should start right now' OR 'Nothing will ever get better, I'm a burden to everyone'

fields.label: 4. How intense is this mood? (0 = barely noticeable, 100 = most intense ever)
5. Which mood-colored thinking pattern might be at work?

options.label: All-or-Nothing — 'Everything is perfect' or 'Everything is hopeless'
Catastrophizing — 'This will never end' / 'Everything is ruined'
Minimizing risk — 'Nothing bad will happen' / 'I don't need help'
Hopelessness — 'Nothing will help' / 'I'll always be like this'
Impulsive reasoning — 'I feel it, so I should act on it now'

fields.label: 6. Evidence for this thought — What supports it?
fields.placeholder: e.g., I do have good ideas sometimes. I have been feeling creative. / I have been struggling for weeks and it's hard.

fields.label: 7. Evidence against — What would I think when my mood is stable?
fields.placeholder: e.g., When stable, I'd plan this over weeks and check my budget first. Past episodes like this led to unfinished projects. / When stable, I know episodes pass and I have recovered before.

fields.label: 8. Balanced thought — What's a more grounded perspective?
fields.placeholder: e.g., 'I may have a good idea, but the urgency I feel right now is a mood symptom. I'll write it down and revisit it in a few days when I can evaluate it more clearly.' / 'I feel terrible right now, but I've felt this way before and it passed. I'll focus on one small thing today.'

fields.label: 9. How intense is the mood now? (0 = barely noticeable, 100 = most intense ever)

distortionsList.name: All-or-Nothing Thinking
distortionsList.description: Seeing things in black and white extremes. During depression: 'Everything is hopeless.' During mania: 'Everything is perfect and I can't fail.' Reality is almost always somewhere in between.

distortionsList.name: Grandiosity
distortionsList.description: Overestimating your abilities, importance, or invulnerability — common during elevated mood. 'I don't need sleep.' 'I'm the only one who can do this.' 'Normal rules don't apply to me right now.' Check: would I think this when my mood is stable?

distortionsList.name: Catastrophizing
distortionsList.description: Assuming the worst possible outcome is certain — common during depression. 'This will never end.' 'My life is over.' Check: is this a fact, or is my depressed mood making it feel like a fact?

distortionsList.name: Minimizing Risk
distortionsList.description: Downplaying consequences and ignoring warning signs — common during elevated mood. 'It'll be fine.' 'I can handle this.' 'What's the worst that could happen?' Check: am I ignoring information I'd normally take seriously?

distortionsList.name: Hopelessness
distortionsList.description: Believing nothing will ever improve — a hallmark of bipolar depression. 'What's the point?' 'I'll always be like this.' Reminder: you have recovered from episodes before. Depression lies about the future.

distortionsList.name: Impulsive Reasoning
distortionsList.description: Treating urgency as proof that action is needed right now. 'I feel like I need to do this immediately.' The feeling of urgency is often a mood symptom, not a genuine signal. Apply the 72-hour rule: if it's truly important, it will still be important in three days.

disclaimer: This thought record is for educational self-reflection, not a substitute for professional therapy. CBT for bipolar disorder is most effective when guided by a trained therapist alongside medication management. If you are experiencing a severe mood episode, contact your treatment team. If you are having thoughts of ending your life, call or text 988 (Suicide & Crisis Lifeline).
